content.content: COVID-19 is a new disease caused by a coronavirus that is spread from one person to another. This illness can range from mild to very serious symptoms and even death.[^250] ## Symptoms:[^251] - Fatigue - Fever &/or chills - Shortness of breath - Constant cough - Headache - Loss of smell or taste - Body aches - Sore throat - Nausea or vomiting - Diarrhea - Stuffy or runny nose Embedded content: https://youtu.be/ZL1z3Uju-I0 __Call 911 if you or someone you know has the following__: - Trouble breathing - Constant chest pain or pressure - New confusion - Difficulty waking up or staying awake - Bluish lips or face ## Adults at Higher Risk:[^252] - 65 years and older - Cancer - Chronic kidney disease - Obesity - Pregnancy - Smoking - Sickle cell disease - Diabetes - Heart disease - Lung disease ## Children at Higher Risk:[^252] - Obesity - Sickle cell disease - Asthma - Congenital heart disease - Chronic kidney disease - Diabetes - Severe neurologic disease - Inherited metabolic disorders - Severe genetic disorders Embedded content: https://youtu.be/4-aloGTYmIc ## Prevention:[^253] - Wear a mask over your nose and mouth. - Stay at least 6 feet away from other people who don’t live with you. - Avoid crowds. - Avoid poorly ventilated indoor spaces. - Wash your hands often with soap & water. - Cover coughs & sneezes. - Clean & disinfect frequently touched surfaces. - Monitor your health daily. - Get the COVID-19 vaccination. Use [Vaccine Finder](https://vaccinefinder.org "Vaccine Finder") to find COVID-19 vaccines in your area. Embedded content: https://youtu.be/dSvff0QljHQ [10 Things You Can Do To Manage Your COVID-19 Symptoms from the CDC](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/downloads/10Things.pdf "10 Things You Can Do To Manage Your COVID-19 Symptoms from the CDC") ## Testing:[^254] - If you have COVID-19 symptoms. - If you have been in close contact with someone who has COVID-19. - If you have been in crowded indoor &/or outdoor places. - If your healthcare provider, [local](https://www.cdc.gov/publichealthgateway/healthdirectories/healthdepartments.html "local") or [state health department](https://www.naccho.org/membership/lhd-directory "state health department") has asked you to get a test. Embedded content: https://youtu.be/K-zS-2KiBmk ## What to Do If You Have COVID-19:[^255] - Stay at home. - Stay away from other people & pets in your home. - Wear a mask over your nose and mouth when you are with other people & pets. - Monitor your symptoms. - Call ahead before visiting your healthcare provider. - Cover your mouth & nose when you cough or sneeze. - Wash your hands often for 20 seconds with soap & water. - Don’t share personal household items. - Clean frequently touched surfaces in your room (& separate bathroom if available). - Even if you don’t feel sick, you can still spread COVID-19. [When You Can be Around Others After You Have Had COVID-19](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/if-you-are-sick/end-home-isolation.html "When You Can be Around Others After You Have Had COVID-19") ## Questions to Ask if Your Loved One is on a Ventilator[^68]: - When can they breathe on their own? - When can the sedation medication be reduced? - Does the patient have pressure sores on their back and/or buttocks? - Can the patient have a subglottic secretion drainage endotracheal tube if the ventilator will be in more than 72 hours? - Are the patient's teeth &/or dentures being cleaned twice a day? - Is the patient's mouth being rinsed with a non-alcohol based mouth rinse? - If the patient is at risk for aspiration will their teeth & mouth be cleaned 2-4 times a day with a suction toothbrush kit? - Is the patient being suctioned every 4 hours? - Is the patient’s head of the bed greater than 30 degrees? - Is everyone that sees the patient washing their hands? - Does the patient have a fever? - Is the patient feeling sick to their stomach? - Is the patient feeling sudden pain? - Does the patient have redness, swelling or tenderness at the airway insertion site? - __Use the Hospital Plan of Care form to advocate and ask questions about your loved one.__ [The Hospital Plan of Care Form](https://patientsafetymovement.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/POC-Blank-April-19.pdf "The Hospital Plan of Care form") [Guide to Medical Care at a Distance](https://patientsafetymovement.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/Guide-to-Medical-Care-at-a-Distance.pdf "Guide to Medical Care at a Distance") ## Vaccines:[^256] Vaccines are one of the best ways to prevent getting COVID-19. Vaccines teach your body how to fight the COVID-19 virus. - Studies show the vaccines are safe and effective. - Vaccines do not contain live virus - Gives you better protection than getting sick with COVID-19. - You can get COVID-19 again even if you’ve had it. - Vaccines will not change your DNA. - Side effects show that the vaccine is working to build your immunity to COVID-19. - Most people with medical conditions should get the vaccine. - You must keep taking steps to prevent COVID-19 after getting the vaccine. ## Side Effects of Vaccine:[^257] - Pain and discomfort at the injection site - Swelling of the skin near injection site - Fever - Fatigue - Body chills - Headache ## Ways to Help Side Effects:[^257] - Put a clean, cold, & wet cloth over the injection site - Drink plenty of fluids - Dress lightly - Rest - Ask your doctor about these over-the-counter medications: - Acetaminophen - Ibuprofen - Aspirin - Antihistamines - Don’t take these medications __before__ getting the vaccine. - Call your doctor if the side effects get worse after 24 to 48 hours. ## Common Questions and Answers:[^257] ### Where can I get the vaccine? - Use [Vaccine Finder](https://vaccinefinder.org "Vaccine Finder") to find COVID-19 vaccines in your area. ### What are the side effects from the vaccine? - Pain and discomfort at the injection site - Swelling of the skin near the injection site - Fever - Fatigue - Body chills - Headache ### If I’m pregnant can I get the vaccine? - Yes, there is no evidence that fertility is affected by the COVID-19 vaccine. - [CDC Information About COVID-19 Vaccines for People Who Are Pregnant or Breastfeeding](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/recommendations/pregnancy.html "CDC Information About COVID-19 Vaccines for People Who Are Pregnant or Breastfeeding") ### Do I need to get the vaccine if I’ve had COVID-19? - Yes, because no one knows how long the protection from the COVID-19 infection will last. - You can get COVID-19 again even if you’ve had it. - If you were infected with COVID-19 and received the monoclonal antibodies or convalescent plasma, it is advised that you wait 90 days before receiving a COVID-19 vaccine. - Ask your doctor about any treatments you may have received when you had COVID-19 and if any of these may affect receiving a COVID-19 vaccine. ### After getting the vaccine, do I need to wear a mask, wash my hands, and social distance? - Yes, you will need to wear a mask, keep 6 feet from others, wash your hands, & avoid crowds inside with poor ventilation. ### If I am sick with the COVID-19 infection, can I still receive a COVID-19 vaccination? - No. If you are sick with the COVID-19 infection, wait to receive a COVID-19 vaccine until you are fully recovered (this includes meeting the criteria for discontinuing self isolation). ### Can I still receive a COVID-19 vaccine with medical conditions? - Individuals with medical conditions are able to receive a COVID-19 vaccine, but they should not have had an immediate or severe allergic reaction to a COVID-19 vaccine or any ingredients in the vaccine. [Ingredient Information for Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/different-vaccines/Pfizer-BioNTech.html) [Ingredient Information for Moderna COVID-19 vaccine](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/different-vaccines/Moderna.html) ### What are the benefits of the vaccine? - COVID-19 vaccines produce protection from infection by allowing the body to develop an immune response to the virus that causes COVID-19. - By developing an immune protection to the SARS Cov2 virus, the chance of getting the COVID-19 infection or developing severe symptoms upon infection are much less. - This immunity will help to fight the infection. - Getting vaccinated can help you, your loved ones, and your community to stop the spread of COVID-19. ### How long will the COVID-19 vaccine protection last? - Experts currently do not know how long protection will last. Receiving a vaccination is a safer choice for decreasing infection for you, your loved ones, and your community. [CDC's Frequently Asked Questions](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/faq.html) Embedded content: https://youtu.be/EILCpte7GSw ## Different Types of Vaccines: - mRNA by Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna - DNA by AstraZeneca and Johnson & Johnson Embedded content: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mvA9gs5gxNY [Ingredient Information for Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/different-vaccines/Pfizer-BioNTech.html) [Ingredient Information for Moderna COVID-19 vaccine](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/different-vaccines/Moderna.html) [COVID-19 Vaccines From the WHO](https://www.who.int/emergencies/diseases/novel-coronavirus-2019/covid-19-vaccines "COVID-19 Vaccines From the WHO") ## Coping with Stress:[^258] - Take breaks from the news and social media. - Eat well. - Care for yourself. - Walk, exercise, meditate, & listen to music. - Stay connected with others. - Get enough sleep. - Relax by doing activities you enjoy. - Avoid excessive alcohol, tobacco, & substance abuse. - Call your healthcare or mental health provider if you are struggling with stress. [Mental Health & Coping During COVID-19 from the CDC](https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/daily-life-coping/managing-stress-anxiety.html?CDC_AA_refVal=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cdc.gov%2Fcoronavirus%2F2019-ncov%2Fprepare%2Fmanaging-stress-anxiety.html) ### Where can I find more information? [WHO Advice for the Public](https://www.who.int/emergencies/diseases/novel-coronavirus-2019/advice-for-public "WHO Advice for the Public") [Hand Washing Guidelines from the CDC](https://www.cdc.gov/handwashing/when-how-handwashing.html) [^250]: Centers for Disease Control & Prevention.
